entries.title: COMMENTARY ON PAUL'S EPISTLES on second-corinthians 8:17
entries.excerpt: Seeing that the Corinthians are making progress in good works, Titus has become particularly concerned about their attitude and has even volunteered to go to visit them.

entries.title: Homily 18 on 2 Corinthians on second-corinthians 8:17
entries.excerpt: Observe how he also represents him as fulfilling his own part, and needing no prompting from others. And having mentioned the grace of God, he doth not leave the whole to be God's; again, that by this also he may win them unto greater love, having said that he was stirred up from himself also. For, "being very earnest, he went forth of his own accord," 'he seized at the thing, he rushed upon the treasure, he considered your service to be his own advantage; and because he loved you exceedingly, he needed not the exhortation I gave; but though he was exhorted by me also, yet it was not by that he was stirred up; but from himself and by the grace of God.'

entries.title: Commentary on 2 Corinthians on second-corinthians 8:17
entries.excerpt: From what is it evident that God stirred up Titus? From the fact that when I asked him, he readily accepted the request, and did not grumble, but set out voluntarily, for even before I asked him, he already had his own zeal.

entries.title: Commentary on 2 Corinthians on second-corinthians 8:17
entries.excerpt: But the sign of this carefulness is that when I asked him, he consented to my exhortation; hence he says, "For he not only accepted our appeal," and because he carried out what I asked; hence he says, but being himself very earnest, of his own accord more than by my exhortation, he is going to you, although at first he refused to go on account of your sins: "Never flag in zeal" (Rom. 12:11).
